0.q: chromosomal mini-Tn10 insertions situated after nucleotide number 997 or 1075 of glnD partially suppressed the osmosensitive phenotype of ΔotsBA or otsA::Tn10 mutations (defective osmoregulatory trehalose synthesis).
sup+ derivatives displayed both strongly increased trehalose-6-phosphate synthase activity and expression of otsA-lacZ and otsB-lacZ operon fusions compared with their parental strains.
overproduction of trehalose (integrated lacI-Ptac-otsBA) above the level produced by the native regulatory system can be used to increase the growth of E. coli in M9-2% glucose medium
These genes code for the two enzymes required for trehalose biosynthesis and E. coli synthesizes trehalose under osmotic stress in order to achieve internal osmotic balance.
Disruption of trehalose synthesis pathway OtsAB completely abolishes trehalose accumulation and greatly suppresses glycogen accumulation.
In E. coli, trehalose-6-phosphate synthase (gene product of otsA) converts UDP-glucose and glucose-6-phosphate to trehalose-6-phosphate.

1.q: The mtlD gene encoding mannitol-1-phosphate dehydrogenase, which catalyzes the biosynthesis of mannitol from fructose... Expression of the gene led to synthesis and accumulation of mannitol in the transgenic plants.
A mannitol biosynthetic gene, mannitol-1-phosphate dehydrogenase (mtlD), from Escherichia coli is known to catalyze mannitol production in various transgenic plants engineered with this gene.
Transgenic tobacco plants that synthesize and accumulate the sugar alcohol mannitol were engineered by introduction of a bacterial gene that encodes mannitol 1-phosphate dehydrogenase.

3.q: SHX was not effective in (p)ppGpp-deﬁcient cells ((p)ppGpp0 cells), indicating the involvement of (p)ppGpp in SHX-induced salt tolerance... MG1655DrelADspoT cells were more susceptible to salt stress than wild-type cells.
pre-activation of these stress responses by a constitutively active relA allele, overexpression of relA or rpoS... confers extreme osmotic shock tolerance similar to rpoB58.

4.q: supplementing with betaine in combination with increased production of endogenous osmoprotectant from a second copy of the trehalose biosynthetic genes (otsBA) improved growth of E. coli and increased the MIC for xylose, glucose, sodium lactate and NaCl.
overproduction of trehalose (integrated lacI-Ptac-otsBA) above the level produced by the native regulatory system can be used to increase the growth of E. coli
marker-free transgenic lines of rice are developed with enhanced trehalose accumulation ... by overexpressing both TPS and TPP as a fusion gene construct

6.q: we constructed an E. coli (p)ppGpp-null strain (MG1655DrelADspoT) by simultaneously disrupting the genes for RelA and SpoT to examine the requirement of (p)ppGpp for drug-induced salt tolerance.
Mutations in the relA and spoT genes, the products of which control (p)ppGpp synthesis to induce the stringent response (SR).

7.q: betA (choline dehydrogenase), betB (betaine aldehyde dehydrogenase)... The expression of these genes was demonstrated in the cyanobacterial cells by northern blot analysis, as well as by the detection of glycine betaine by 1H NMR in cells supplemented with choline.
a 62 kDa membrane-associated choline dehydrogenase (CDH) first oxidizes choline into betaine aldehyde. CDH is also able to catalyse the second step, oxidation of betaine aldehyde into betaine.

8.q: The second step, conversion of betaine aldehyde into glycine betaine, can also be performed by the second enzyme in the pathway, betaine aldehyde dehydrogenase (BADH), encoded by betB.
betA (choline dehydrogenase), betB (betaine aldehyde dehydrogenase)... detection of glycine betaine by 1H nuclear magnetic resonance in cells supplemented with choline.

9.q: Osmotic activation of ProP is attenuated 5-fold in the absence of soluble protein ProQ, but proQ lesions do not influence proP transcription or ProP levels.
Lesions at proQ decrease the level of the osmoregulatory glycine betaine transporter ProP.

11.q: the katE transgene increased the resistance of the chloroplast's translational machinery to salt stress by scavenging hydrogen peroxide.

12.q: The betA gene, from Escherichia coli, encodes choline dehydrogenase (CDH), which could catalyze the synthesis of betaine from choline by a two-step oxidation.

13.q: Co-precipitation of proteins interacting with the C-terminal YbdG region led to the isolation of HldD and FbaA, whose overexpression in cells containing the YbdG-I167T variant partially rescued the osmo-sensitive phenotype.

14.q: Co-precipitation of proteins interacting with the C-terminal YbdG region led to the isolation of HldD and FbaA, whose overexpression in cells containing the YbdG-I167T variant partially rescued the osmo-sensitive phenotype.

15.q: GalU mutants, defective in synthesis of UDP-glucose, did not accumulate trehalose.

16.q: A CRP binding-site mutation or the absence of a functional crp gene leads to high constitutive expression of P1. We show that the binding of CRP-cAMP inhibits transcription by purified RNA polymerase in vitro at P1.

17.q: steady-state levels of proP mRNA and ProP protein are inversely correlated with cellular RNase III activity and, in turn, affect the proline uptake capacity of the cell.

18.q: the much-studied glnD99::Tn10 mutation ... causes a complete repression of glnKp-lacZ expression under all growth conditions

19.q: The promoter region of narUZYWV TU was extensively occupied by OmpR under the osmotic stress condition and this binding significantly increased transcript levels under this condition.

20.q: mannitol can be synthesized from fructose-6 phosphate via the sequential enzymatic reactions catalyzed by mannitol-1-phosphate dehydrogenase (mtlD) and mannitol-1-phosphatase (m1p)
ectopic expression of the mtlD gene for the biosynthesis of mannitol in wheat improves tolerance to water stress and salinity.
Hema et al. (2014) transferred the mannitol-1-phosphate dehydrogenase gene (mtlD) into finger millet, enhancing its drought resistance and salt tolerance.

21.q: GutD gene, encoding a key enzyme (glucitol-6-phosphate dehydrogenase) of sugar alcohol metabolic pathway in E. coli, was transferred into maize.
Yan et al. (1999) transferred the glucitol-6-phosphate dehydrogenase gene (gutD) into the maize genome to increase its salt tolerance.

22.q: tobacco plants were transformed with a bacterial gene (AroG) encode to feedback-insensitive 3-deoxy-D-arabino-heptulosonate 7-phosphate synthase, the first enzyme of the shikimate pathway.

23.q: Luciferase reporter gene assays demonstrated that osmotic stress caused by carbohydrates led to the expression of ahpCF and dps, which was not observed in an E. coli ΔoxyR mutant.

24.q: E. coli strains containing chaA were able to adapt to conditions of high KCl concentration and alkalinity more easily and were able to grow under these conditions at least two times faster than strains lacking chaA.

25.q: growth arrest did not occur in cells lacking the enzyme of cAMP synthesis or cAMP receptor. We found that the stress-induced growth arrest disappeared when the gene for cAMP synthesis (cyaA) or its receptor protein (crp) was deleted.

26.q: OmpR coordinates genome-scale transcriptional responses to osmotic stress with its cognate sensor EnvZ in many enteric bacteria.

27.q: The expression of E. coli gutD gene led to the synthesis and accumulation of sugar alcohol sorbitol (concentration: (0.52 ± 0.19) μmol/g fwt).

28.q: The results indicate that the expression of E. coli gutD gene led to the synthesis and accumulation of sugar alcohol sorbitol.

29.q: MG1655DrsgADrelADspoT cells showed signiﬁcantly better growth than did the MG1655DrelADspoT cells after salt shock, indicating that salt tolerance induced by depletion of RsgA does not require (p)ppGpp.

30.q: Both lacked the trkH homologue trkG.

31.q: both strains grew in high osmolality medium at low K+ (micromolar concentrations) and retained locus trkH, which encodes an osmoregulatory K+ transporter.

32.q: Oxidative stress response proteins such as AhpF, Dps, and Fur, all of which belong to the oxyR regulon, were upregulated in E. coli isolates from mice fed the lactose-rich diet.

33.q: Oxidative stress response proteins such as AhpF, Dps, and Fur, all of which belong to the oxyR regulon, were upregulated in E. coli isolates from mice fed the lactose-rich diet.

34.q: addition of exogenous cAMP had no effects on the growth of ∆crp cells, indicating that both cAMP and CRP are required for growth arrest.

35.q: GlnD of E. coli is a bifunctional signal-transducing enzyme which uridylylates the allosteric regulatory protein PII and deuridylylates PII-UMP in response to growth with nitrogen excess or limitation, respectively.

36.q: KduI facilitated the breakdown of these hexuronates... These results indicate a novel function of KduI and KduD in E. coli

37.q: KduI facilitated the breakdown of these hexuronates... These results indicate a novel function of KduI and KduD in E. coli

38.q: GlnD ... indirectly directs the expression of nitrogen-regulated genes, e.g., the glnK-amtB operon.

39.q: glyoxalase III (GLY III) catalyzes the same conversion of MG to D-lactate in a single step in GSH-independent manner.

40.q: The caiT gene forms part of the caiTABCDE operon which encodes the carnitine pathway, and is transcribed during anaerobic growth in the presence of carnitine.

44.q: Additional genetic experiments showed that enhancing the expression of the mal regulon, especially for genes that encode the glycoporin LamB and maltose transporter, contributed to the osmotolerance phenotype.

45.q: the binding of the cyclic AMP (cAMP) receptor protein (CRP) to a site centered at -34.5 within the promoter is responsible for the low promoter activity... A CRP binding-site mutation or the absence of a functional crp gene leads to high constitutive expression of P1.
